results.title_inferred: Rhubarb (Daio / Da Huang) — Honzō Wamyō Entry
results.original_text: 大黄 (Daio / Da Huang) — Rheum palmatum / R. officinale. Honzo Wamyo entry: 大黄、和名 加波良久佐。性寒、味苦。主下瘀血、血閉、寒熱、破癥瘕積聚、留飲宿食、蕩滌腸胃、推陳致新、通利水穀、調中化食、安和五臓。Daio, Japanese name Kawarakusa. Nature: cold. Taste: bitter. Primary uses: dispels static blood, opens blocked blood, treats alternating cold and heat, breaks up accumulations and masses, dissolves retained fluids and old food, flushes and purges the intestines and stomach, eliminates the old and generates the new, facilitates passage of water and grain, regulates the middle and transforms food, calms and harmonises the five viscera. Preparation: root decocted 3-12g; reduce cooking time to preserve cathartic effect. Kampo use: Daio-kanzoto formula (rhubarb + licorice) for constipation. Contraindicated in pregnancy and weakness.
results.translation: Rhubarb [大黄, Daio / Da Huang] — Japanese vernacular name: Kawarakusa [加波良久佐]. Thermal nature: cold. Flavour: bitter. Primary therapeutic actions: dispels static [stagnant] blood [瘀血, oketsu], opens blockage of blood, treats patterns of alternating cold and heat [寒熱, kannetsu], breaks up accumulations and masses [癥瘕積聚, chōka-shakkyo], dissolves retained fluids and old undigested food [留飲宿食], flushes and purges [蕩滌, tōteki] the intestines and stomach, eliminates the old and generates the new [推陳致新], facilitates the passage of water and grain [water metabolism and digestion], regulates the middle [burner] and transforms food, and calms and harmonises the five viscera [五臓, gozō]. Preparation: the root is decocted in doses of 3–12 g; cooking time should be kept short in order to preserve the cathartic [purgative] effect. Use in Kampo [Japanese classical medicine]: the formula Daio-kanzoto [大黄甘草湯, rhubarb combined with licorice root] is employed for constipation. Contraindicated during pregnancy and in patients presenting with deficiency [weakness].

entities_mentioned.name: 大黄 / Daio / Da Huang (Kawarakusa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. and/or Rheum officinale Baill. (Chinese / Turkish rhubarb)
entities_mentioned.notes: The source text explicitly supplies the binomial identifications Rheum palmatum and R. officinale. The Japanese vernacular Kawarakusa (加波良久佐) appears in the Honzō Wamyō, a 10th-century Japanese materia medica compiled by Minamoto no Shitagō. The root (rhizome) is the medicinal part used.

entities_mentioned.name: Daio-kanzoto (大黄甘草湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kampo formula: Rheum palmatum root + Glycyrrhiza uralensis (licorice) root
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Kampo compound formula prescribed primarily for constipation. Corresponds to the Chinese classical formula Da Huang Gan Cao Tang from the Jinkui Yaolue.

entities_mentioned.name: Licorice (甘草, Kanzo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Named only as a component of the Daio-kanzoto formula; not treated as a standalone entry in this segment.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a hybrid document: it interleaves classical Chinese (kanbun) materia medica text drawn from the Honzō Wamyō with modern editorial annotations (binomial identification, dosage guidance, Kampo formula, contraindications). These layers have been rendered together in the translation but are distinguishable: the kanbun portion ends with 安和五臓, after which the remaining content is modern editorial commentary. The term 推陳致新 (eliminate the old, generate the new) is a classical pharmacological formula phrase describing rhubarb's cathartic-regenerative action and has been retained in brackets. Dosage range of 3–12 g reflects standard modern Kampo/TCM practice. The contraindication in pregnancy is well-attested in both historical and contemporary sources.

results.title_inferred: Kanzo (Licorice Root) — Glycyrrhiza uralensis in Honzō Wamyō
results.original_text: 甘草 (Kanzo / Gan Cao) — Glycyrrhiza uralensis. 甘草、和名 阿末加都良。甘、平。主五臓六腑寒熱邪気、堅筋骨、長肌肉、倍力、金創腫、解毒。久服輕身延年。
results.translation: Kanzo [甘草, 'sweet herb'], Japanese name Amakatsura [阿末加都良]. Flavour: sweet; temperament [qi]: neutral [平]. Indicated for cold and heat pathogenic factors [寒熱邪気] in the five viscera [五臓] and six bowels [六腑]; strengthens sinews and bones [筋骨]; builds flesh and muscle [肌肉]; multiplies strength [倍力]; treats swelling from metal wounds [金創腫, i.e. lacerations from bladed implements]; antidotes poisons [解毒]. Long-term use lightens the body [輕身] and extends the years of life [延年]. Kanzo is the most frequently employed herb across all of Kampo [Japanese classical medicine], appearing in approximately 70% of compound formulae in the role of harmoniser [調和] and moderator of the actions of other constituent herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 / Kanzo / Gan Cao / Amakatsura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (Chinese licorice root; family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification with Glycyrrhiza uralensis is explicit in the source label and is consistent with standard Kampo and Chinese materia medica scholarship. The Japanese vernacular name Amakatsura (阿末加都良) is an archaic phonetic rendering recorded in the Honzō Wamyō (本草和名, c. 918 CE). The active constituents include glycyrrhizin and liquiritin, which underpin the documented anti-inflammatory, antitoxic, and harmonising effects referenced in the classical indications.

results.translator_notes: The term 金創腫 (lit. 'metal-wound swelling') refers to injuries caused by metal implements such as swords or knives — a standard classical category; rendered here as 'swelling from metal wounds' to preserve the period specificity. The phrase 輕身延年 ('lightens the body and extends life') is a conventional Daoist-inflected longevity formula appearing across many entries in the Shennong Bencao Jing tradition and should not be read as a literal pharmacological claim. The statistic that Kanzo appears in ~70% of Kampo compound formulae is an editorial note in the source segment, not a direct translation of classical text; it has been retained in the translation but is flagged here as a modern editorial interpolation rather than a primary-source statement.

results.title_inferred: Keishi (Cinnamon Twig): Uses in Classical Japanese and Kampo Medicine
results.original_text: 桂枝 (Keishi / Gui Zhi) — Cinnamomum cassia (young twigs). Honzo Wamyo: 桂枝、和名 加爾。辛、温。主上気咳逆、結気喉痺吐吸、利関節、補中益気。Keishi, Japanese name Kani. Pungent, warm. Primary uses: descends rebellious qi, stops coughing, opens constricted qi in throat, facilitates movement in the joints, tonifies the middle and benefits qi. Yoshimasu Todo (18th c. Koho school): Keishi is the primary herb for dispelling cold from the surface and warming the channels. Keishi-bukuryo-gan formula (cinnamon + hoelen fungus): documented Kampo treatment for pelvic inflammatory conditions and menstrual disorders with multiple modern clinical trials.
results.translation: Keishi [桂枝, also romanized from Chinese as Gui Zhi] — the young twigs of Cinnamomum cassia. According to the Honzō Wamyō [本草和名, a classical Japanese materia medica]: 'Keishi; Japanese name, Kani. [Flavor:] pungent; [nature:] warm. Principal indications: counterflow ascent of qi [上気] with coughing and rebellious breath; constricted qi [結気] causing throat obstruction [喉痺] with difficult respiration; promotes free movement in the joints; tonifies the middle burner and augments qi.' Yoshimasu Tōdō (18th century, Kohō school of Kampo): Keishi is the foremost herb for expelling cold from the body's exterior and warming the channels and vessels. The formula Keishi-bukuryō-gan [桂枝茯苓丸] — combining cinnamon twig with Poria (hoelen fungus) — is a documented Kampo compound preparation used for pelvic inflammatory conditions and menstrual disorders, and has been the subject of multiple modern clinical trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Keishi / 桂枝 (Gui Zhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Nees) T.Nees ex Blume — young twigs (syn. Cinnamomum aromaticum); family Lauraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Keishi specifically denotes the young twigs, distinguishing it from Keihi [桂皮], which denotes the bark. The Japanese name 'Kani' (加爾) is an archaic reading recorded in the Honzō Wamyō.

entities_mentioned.name: Honzō Wamyō (本草和名)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Japanese pharmaceutical/botanical dictionary, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito, ca. 918 CE. One of the earliest Japanese materia medica texts, drawing heavily on Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Yoshimasu Tōdō (吉益東洞)
entities_mentioned.notes: Influential Japanese physician (1702–1773), founder of the Kohō (古方) school of Kampo, which advocated return to Han-dynasty Chinese classical formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Keishi-bukuryō-gan (桂枝茯苓丸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Kampo compound formula; ingredients include Cinnamomum cassia twig, Poria cocos (hoelen), Paeonia lactiflora, Prunus persica (peach kernel), and Paeonia suffruticosa (moutan bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Originating in the Jinkui Yaolue [金匱要略] (Zhang Zhongjing, Han dynasty). Used in modern Kampo practice for gynecological blood-stasis conditions. Subject of clinical research in Japan regarding uterine fibroids and dysmenorrhea.

entities_mentioned.name: Poria / Hoelen (Bukuryo / 茯苓)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf; family Polyporaceae — sclerotium of a parasitic fungus
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as 'hoelen fungus' in the source text. Standard Kampo ingredient with diuretic and calming properties according to traditional theory.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a mixed-language scholarly note combining classical Chinese/Japanese characters with modern English editorial commentary; the classical Japanese passage from the Honzō Wamyō has been translated faithfully according to standard Kampo terminology. The term '吐吸' (lit. 'exhaling and inhaling') in context of '喉痺吐吸' indicates labored or obstructed respiration associated with throat bi-obstruction syndrome, rendered here as 'difficult respiration.' The segment label identifies this as part of the Materia Medica Americana project, though keishi is an East Asian drug with no indigenous American provenance; this may reflect comparative or transplant pharmacognosy scope within the archive.

results.title_inferred: Ma Huang (Ephedra) — Honzo Wamyo Entry with Kampo Clinical Notes
results.original_text: 麻黄 (Mao / Ma Huang) — Ephedra sinica. Honzo Wamyo: 麻黄、和名 宇末乃久佐。苦、温。主中風傷寒頭痛、温瘧、發表出汗、去邪熱気、止咳逆上気、除寒熱、破癥堅積聚。Mao (Ephedra), Japanese name Umanokusa. Bitter, warm. Indicated for wind-cold with headache, warm malaria, promotes sweating to dispel exterior pathogens, clears fever-heat, stops coughing and rebellious qi, dispels alternating cold and heat, breaks up hard accumulations. Kampo clinical refinement: Yoshimasu Todo noted that Japanese patients required lower doses than Chinese classical texts specified — the dosing was refined empirically over centuries. Ephedrine isolated from ma huang by Nagai Nagayoshi 1885.
results.translation: Ma Huang [麻黄] — Japanese name: Umanokusa [宇末乃久佐]. Flavor: bitter; Temperament: warm. Principal indications: wind-cold [中風傷寒] with headache; warm malaria [温瘧]; promotion of sweating to expel exterior pathogens [發表出汗]; clearing of febrile heat [邪熱気]; arrest of coughing and rebellious rising qi [咳逆上気]; dispelling of alternating cold and heat [寒熱]; and breaking up of hard masses and accumulations [癥堅積聚]. Kampo clinical refinement: The physician Yoshimasu Todo observed that Japanese patients required lower doses than those prescribed in Chinese classical medical texts, and dosing was adjusted empirically over many generations of practice. The alkaloid ephedrine was first isolated from ma huang by the Japanese chemist Nagai Nagayoshi in 1885.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻黄 / Ma Huang / Mao / Umanokusa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (family Ephedraceae); source of the alkaloid ephedrine
entities_mentioned.notes: The Japanese vernacular name Umanokusa (宇末乃久佐, literally 'horse grass') appears in the Honzo Wamyo, a 10th-century Japanese pharmacopoeia. The plant is used as a diaphoretic, antipyretic, antitussive, and for breaking up internal accumulations.

entities_mentioned.name: Yoshimasu Todo
entities_mentioned.notes: Prominent 18th-century Japanese Kampo physician (1702–1773) known for empirical revision of classical Chinese dosing protocols to suit Japanese clinical practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Nagai Nagayoshi
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese chemist (1844–1929) credited with the first isolation of ephedrine from Ephedra sinica in 1885, a landmark in pharmacognosy.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedrine; sympathomimetic alkaloid (C₁₀H₁₅NO)
entities_mentioned.notes: Active constituent of ma huang responsible for bronchodilatory, vasopressor, and diaphoretic effects; isolation in 1885 by Nagai Nagayoshi provided a chemical basis for the plant's classical indications.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a hybrid document combining: (1) the original Classical Chinese pharmacopoeial entry from the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, ca. 918 CE) with its Japanese reading gloss; (2) an embedded English paraphrase of those classical indications; and (3) editorial annotations on Kampo dosing history and modern phytochemistry. These layers have been preserved and clearly separated in the translation. The term 'rebellious qi' [逆上気] is standard Sino-Japanese medical terminology denoting pathological upward counter-flow of breath or vital force, typically manifesting as coughing or panting. 'Hard accumulations' [癥堅積聚] refers to palpable abdominal masses recognized in classical East Asian medicine. The segment label 'honzo_wamyo::mao_ephedra' accurately identifies the primary source.

results.title_inferred: Oren (Coptis) — Properties, Uses, and Preparations
results.original_text: 黄連 (Oren / Huang Lian) — Coptis japonica / C. chinensis. Honzo Wamyo: 黄連、和名 加加美久佐。苦、寒。主熱気目痛、眥傷泣出、明目、腸澼腹痛下痢、婦人陰中腫痛。Oren, Japanese name Kakamikusa. Bitter, cold. Primary: heat conditions with eye pain and lacrimation, brightens vision, intestinal flux with abdominal pain and diarrhea, women's internal swelling and pain. Active compound: berberine (ベルベリン) — antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, blood glucose regulation. Oren-gedoku-to formula: widely used in Japanese medicine for inflammatory conditions. Modern research: berberine shows clinical evidence comparable to metformin for blood glucose management in Type 2 diabetes.
results.translation: Oren [黄連; Chinese: Huang Lian] — Japanese name: Kakamikusa [加加美久佐]. Taste: bitter. Thermal quality: cold. Primary indications: heat conditions accompanied by eye pain and damage to the canthi [眥傷] with lacrimation; brightens the eyes [明目]; intestinal flux [腸澼] with abdominal pain and diarrhea; internal genital swelling and pain in women [婦人陰中腫痛]. The active compound, berberine, has demonstrated antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and blood glucose-regulating properties. The compound formula Oren-gedoku-to [黄連解毒湯; 'Coptis Detoxifying Decoction'] is widely employed in Japanese traditional medicine for inflammatory conditions. Modern clinical research indicates that berberine exhibits efficacy comparable to metformin in the management of blood glucose in Type 2 diabetes.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄連 (Oren / Huang Lian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica and/or Coptis chinensis (family Ranunculaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Source text explicitly provides binomial identification. Japanese vernacular name given as Kakamikusa (加加美久佐). Rhizome is the medicinally used part. Rich in the isoquinoline alkaloid berberine.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine (ベルベリン)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Berberine; isoquinoline alkaloid, CAS 2086-83-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified in source text as the principal active compound. Modern pharmacological evidence supports antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antidiabetic (insulin-sensitizing) activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Oren-gedoku-to (黄連解毒湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Japanese/Chinese compound formula: Coptis Decoction to Relieve Toxicity
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Kampo formula composed of Coptis rhizome, Phellodendron bark, Gardenia fruit, and Scutellaria root. Used for inflammatory and febrile conditions. Preparation method: decoction.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a composite entry blending classical Honzo Wamyo [本草和名] text with modern pharmacological annotation. The classical section provides the original Chinese materia medica indications in kanbun; the modern section adds phytochemical and clinical research commentary. These layers are distinct in register and period and have been rendered accordingly in the translation. The term 腸澼 (chōheki / cháng pì) is translated as 'intestinal flux,' following standard historical materia medica convention for dysenteric/diarrheal conditions with mucus or blood. The term 眥傷 refers specifically to injury or irritation of the canthus (corner of the eye). No genuinely illegible passages were encountered.

results.title_inferred: Bofu (Siler Root / Saposhnikovia divaricata) — Honzō Wamyō Entry
results.original_text: 防風 (Bofu / Fang Feng) — Saposhnikovia divaricata. Honzo Wamyo entry documenting this as a major Kampo herb for wind-type conditions. 防風、和名 加是久佐。甘、温。主大風頭眩痛、悪風風邪、目盲無所見、風行周身骨節疼痺煩満。
results.translation: Bōfū [防風; Chinese: Fáng Fēng], Japanese vernacular name: Kazekusa [加是久佐, lit. 'wind-grass']. Flavour: sweet. Thermal quality: warm. Principally indicated for: major wind disease [大風] with dizziness and pain of the head; aversion to wind and wind-type pathogenic factors [悪風風邪]; blindness and inability to see [目盲無所見]; wind permeating the entire body causing pain, obstruction [痺], and fullness [煩満] of the bone joints. A prominent compound formula employing this herb is Bōfū-tsūshō-san, which remains among the most widely prescribed Kampo formulas in contemporary Japan, used clinically for obesity, metabolic syndrome, and constipation, and which has been the subject of multiple randomised controlled trials.

entities_mentioned.name: 防風 (Bōfū / Fáng Fēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Trucz.) Schischk. (family Apiaceae); root used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in older Western herbalism as 'Siler'; the Japanese vernacular name Kazekusa (風草, 'wind-grass') reflects its classical association with wind-type [風] conditions in East Asian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Bōfū-tsūshō-san (防風通聖散)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A compound Kampo formula containing Saposhnikovia divaricata root as a principal ingredient, along with approximately seventeen additional herbs
entities_mentioned.notes: Not part of the Honzō Wamyō source text itself; added as contextual annotation in the source document. Modern clinical use for obesity and metabolic syndrome is supported by RCT evidence. Translators should note this is editorial supplementation, not primary-source content.

results.translator_notes: The segment conflates two distinct layers: (1) the primary-source Honzō Wamyō [本草和名] entry in classical Chinese/Sino-Japanese, and (2) modern editorial annotation regarding Bōfū-tsūshō-san and clinical research. These layers have been preserved in the translation but distinguished in context. The classical indications (dizziness, joint pain, wind-obstruction, blindness) follow the standard Shennong Bencao Jing / Bencao tradition and are reproduced faithfully. The term 痺 [hi/bi] is rendered 'obstruction' following its standard Kampo gloss of impaired circulation or sensation in the limbs. 煩満 is rendered 'fullness with oppression/unease' condensed to 'fullness' for readability.

results.title_inferred: Shakuyaku (Peony Root) — Paeonia lactiflora in Honzō Wamyō
results.original_text: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku / Shao Yao) — Paeonia lactiflora. Honzo Wamyo: 芍薬、和名 恵比須久佐。苦、平。主邪気腹痛、除血痺、破堅積、寒熱疝瘕、止痛、利小便、益気。Shakuyaku, Japanese name Ebisukusa. Bitter, neutral. Main uses: pathogenic factor causing abdominal pain, dispels blood obstruction, breaks up hard accumulations, alternating cold and heat with shan conglomeration, stops pain, promotes urination, benefits qi. Active compound: paeoniflorin — muscle relaxant, anti-spasmodic, anti-inflammatory. Shakuyaku-kanzo-to (peony + licorice): remarkable anti-spasmodic formula, RCT evidence for muscle cramps and dysmenorrhea. One of most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas.
results.translation: Shakuyaku [芍薬], Japanese vernacular name Ebisukusa [恵比須久佐]. Taste: bitter; temperament [qi nature]: neutral [平, ping]. Principal indications: pathogenic qi [邪気] causing abdominal pain; dispels blood obstruction [血痺, blood impediment/stasis]; breaks up hard accumulations [堅積]; alternating cold and heat associated with shan [疝] conglomeration [瘕, mobile or fixed abdominal masses]; stops pain; promotes urination; augments qi [益気]. — The active constituent paeoniflorin acts as a muscle relaxant, anti-spasmodic, and anti-inflammatory agent. The compound Kampo formula Shakuyaku-kanzō-tō (peony root combined with licorice root) is a well-regarded anti-spasmodic preparation, supported by randomized controlled trial [RCT] evidence for the treatment of muscle cramps and dysmenorrhea, and is among the most frequently prescribed formulas in contemporary Kampo [classical Japanese herbal] medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 芍薬 / Shakuyaku / Shao Yao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (Chinese peony; root used medicinally)
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese vernacular name given as Ebisukusa [恵比須久佐]. Standard medicinal peony of East Asian pharmacopoeias. Root is the officinal part.

entities_mentioned.name: Paeoniflorin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeoniflorin (monoterpene glycoside, CAS 23180-57-6), principal bioactive constituent of Paeonia lactiflora root
entities_mentioned.notes: Documented muscle relaxant, anti-spasmodic, and anti-inflammatory properties in modern pharmacological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Shakuyaku-kanzō-tō
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kampo formula composed of Paeonia lactiflora root (shakuyaku) and Glycyrrhiza uralensis / G. glabra root (kanzō, licorice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical anti-spasmodic formula. RCT-level evidence cited for efficacy in muscle cramps and dysmenorrhea. One of the most commonly prescribed Kampo formulas in Japan.

entities_mentioned.name: Kanzō (licorice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or Glycyrrhiza glabra L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only as the paired ingredient in Shakuyaku-kanzō-tō; not the primary subject of this segment.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a hybrid text combining a classical Chinese/Japanese pharmacopoeia entry (Honzō Wamyō, 本草和名, c. 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito) with modern pharmacological annotations added by a later editor or compiler. The classical portion (苦、平。主邪気腹痛…) follows the standard format of early East Asian materia medica entries and closely parallels the Shennong Bencao Jing tradition. The term 疝瘕 (shan-jia) refers to a classical diagnostic category encompassing mobile or fixed lower abdominal masses/conglomerations, often associated with hernial or gynecological pathology; translated here as 'shan conglomeration' to preserve technical precision. The annotations on paeoniflorin and RCT evidence are clearly modern interpolations and are not part of the original Honzō Wamyō text.

results.title_inferred: Poria (Bukuryo / Fu Ling): Medicinal Fungal Sclerotium from Pine Roots
results.original_text: 茯苓 (Bukuryo / Fu Ling) — Poria cocos (sclerotium of fungus growing on pine roots). Honzo Wamyo: 茯苓、和名 末都保止。甘、平。主胸脇逆気、憂恚驚邪恐悸、心下結痛、寒熱煩満咳逆、口焦舌乾、利小便。Bukuryo, Japanese name Matsuhoji (pine-holding). Sweet, neutral. Indicated for rebellious qi in the chest and ribs, worry, anger, fright, pathogenic factors, fearfulness and palpitations, painful binding below the heart, alternating cold and heat with irritability and fullness, cough with rebellious qi, dry mouth and tongue, promotes urination. Widely used Kampo sedative-calming agent. Active: pachymic acid, beta-glucans — immune modulating, anxiolytic. Keishi-bukuryo-gan: one of most clinically studied Kampo formulas, evidence for uterine fibroids, endometriosis.
results.translation: Poria [茯苓, Japanese: Bukuryo; Chinese: Fu Ling] — the sclerotium of a fungus (Poria cocos) growing on pine roots. From the Honzō Wamyō [本草和名, a Japanese materia medica]: 'Bukuryo, Japanese name Matsuhoji [末都保止, meaning "pine-holding"]. Flavor: sweet; nature: neutral [balanced, neither warming nor cooling]. Principal indications: rebellious qi [counterflow of vital energy] in the chest and ribcage; worry, anger, fright, invasion by pathogenic factors, fearfulness, and palpitations; painful binding sensation below the heart; alternating cold and heat with irritability and fullness; cough with rebellious qi [upward counterflow]; dry mouth and parched tongue; promotes urination [diuretic action].' It is widely employed in Kampo [Japanese traditional medicine] as a sedative and calming agent. Known active constituents include pachymic acid and beta-glucans, which demonstrate immunomodulating and anxiolytic properties. The compound formula Keishi-bukuryo-gan [Cinnamon Twig and Poria Pill] is among the most extensively studied Kampo preparations, with clinical evidence supporting its use in uterine fibroids and endometriosis.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bukuryo / Fu Ling)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf — dried sclerotium; family Polyporaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The sclerotium forms on the roots of Pinus species. Long-established identity in both Chinese and Japanese materia medica traditions. Active constituents pachymic acid and beta-glucans are pharmacologically documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Keishi-bukuryo-gan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan — Cinnamon Twig and Poria Pill (compound Kampo/TCM formula)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical compound formula containing Poria among other ingredients. Cited here in the context of modern clinical evidence for gynecological conditions (uterine fibroids, endometriosis).

entities_mentioned.name: Pachymic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A lanostane-type triterpenoid isolated from Poria cocos
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified as one of the principal bioactive constituents; noted for anxiolytic and immunomodulating activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-glucans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polysaccharide constituents (β-1,3/1,6-glucans) from Poria cocos
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-documented immunomodulating compounds found in fungal cell walls; noted here specifically in the context of Poria cocos.

results.title_inferred: Saikou (Bupleurum): Properties, Indications, and Uses in Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 柴胡、和名 美之末。苦、平。主心腹腸胃中結気、飲食積聚、寒熱邪気、推陳致新。久服輕身明目益精。
results.translation: Saikou [柴胡, Chai Hu], Japanese name Mishima [美之末]. Flavor: bitter; nature: neutral [平, balanced/temperate]. Primary indications: constricted qi [結気] in the region of the heart, abdomen, intestines, and stomach; accumulation and stagnation of food and drink [飲食積聚]; alternating cold and heat due to pathogenic qi [寒熱邪気]; eliminates the stale and promotes the new [推陳致新]. Prolonged use lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and replenishes essential vitality [益精].

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 (Saikou / Chai Hu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum (sickle-leaf hare's ear) or related Bupleurum spp., family Apiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard identification in Sino-Japanese botanical medicine. The Japanese vernacular name given is Mishima [美之末], likely referring to a regional or trade name. Saikosaponins are the principal bioactive constituents and have been the subject of modern pharmacological research for hepatoprotective and immunomodulatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Sho-saiko-to (小柴胡湯, Minor Bupleurum Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula containing Bupleurum root as chief herb, combined with Scutellaria, Pinellia, Ginger, Jujube, Ginseng, and Licorice
entities_mentioned.notes: This formula and its modern clinical study data appear to be editorial annotations added to the classical Honzo Wamyo source text, not part of the original historical document. They should be treated as secondary commentary rather than primary historical material.

The JSON translation above preserves only the classical text as the 'original_text' field. The modern commentary, while accurate in substance, is not part of the primary source and should not be attributed to the Honzo Wamyo. The condition treated corresponds to what classical Chinese medicine terms Shaoyang-pattern disorders (alternating chills and fever, digestive stagnation). Preparation method is not specified in this excerpt beyond the implied decoction (煎) standard for root medicines of this class.

results.title_inferred: Huang Qi / Ougi (Astragalus membranaceus) — Properties, Indications, and Modern Research
results.original_text: 黄耆 (Ougi / Huang Qi) — Astragalus membranaceus. Honzo Wamyo: 黄耆、和名 木乃美奈。甘、微温。主癰疽久敗瘡、排膿止痛、大風癩疾、五痔鼠瘻、補虚小児百病。
results.translation: Huang Qi (Ougi), Japanese vernacular name: Konomi-na [木乃美奈]. Flavor: sweet; thermal quality: mildly warm. Primary indications: carbuncles [癰] and deep abscesses [疽] with chronically festering sores [久敗瘡]; promotes drainage of pus and alleviates pain; serious wind disease [大風] and leprous conditions [癩疾]; the five types of hemorrhoids [五痔]; mouse-track fistulas [鼠瘻, i.e., scrofulous sinus tracts]; supplements vacuity [補虛]; and the full range of children's diseases [小児百病]. In modern Kampo practice, this herb is regarded as a principal immune-tonifying agent. Bioactive constituents include astragalosides and polysaccharides. Active research areas encompass immune modulation, adjunct therapy in oncology, anti-aging effects (notably telomere-related mechanisms), and cardiac protection following myocardial infarction, the last primarily attributed to astragaloside IV.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄耆 / Ougi / Huang Qi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge (family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most extensively documented tonic herbs in East Asian medicine. The Japanese vernacular name Konomi-na [木乃美奈] appears in the Honzō Wamyō (本草和名, c. 918 CE). Modern pharmacological research focuses on astragaloside IV, cycloastragenol, and immunomodulatory polysaccharides.

entities_mentioned.name: 鼠瘻 (mouse-track sores / rat-hole fistulas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofulous or tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis with sinus tract formation
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese medical term for sinuous subcutaneous fistulas, traditionally associated with pathological Qi stagnation and toxic accumulation; often corresponds in modern terms to scrofula or chronic lymph node fistulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 五痔 (five types of hemorrhoids)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various anorectal conditions classified in classical Chinese medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical typology of five hemorrhoidal conditions (牡痔, 牝痔, 脈痔, 腸痔, 血痔) encompasses what modern medicine would categorize as internal and external hemorrhoids and related anorectal pathologies.

entities_mentioned.name: 大風癩疾 (serious wind disease / leprosy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leprosy (Mycobacterium leprae infection) or severe chronic dermatological disease
entities_mentioned.notes: 大風 in classical texts frequently refers to Hansen's disease (leprosy); 癩疾 reinforces this reading, though the term was also applied broadly to severe, disfiguring skin conditions.

results.title_inferred: Shimotsu-to (Four Substance Decoction): Formula, Components, and Clinical Commentary
results.original_text: 四物湯 (Shimotsu-to / Si Wu Tang) — Four Substance Decoction. One of the foundational blood-tonifying formulas in Kampo. Components: 当帰 (Toki/Dang Gui, Angelica sinensis) 12g + 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Shao Yao, Paeonia lactiflora) 12g + 川芎 (Senkyu/Chuan Xiong, Ligusticum chuanxiong) 8g + 地黄 (Jio/Di Huang, Rehmannia glutinosa) 16g. Yoshimasu Todo on Shimotsu-to: This formula addresses blood deficiency patterns manifesting as pale complexion, dizziness, palpitations, menstrual irregularity, and dry skin. It was refined in Japan to use different proportions than the original Chinese formula based on clinical observation. The Koho school's emphasis on direct clinical observation and formula modification is documented in Yoshimasu Todo's Yakucho and Ruijuho. Modern research on individual components: Angelica sinensis (ferulic acid) — anti-platelet, blood-moving; Rehmannia — blood sugar regulation; Ligusticum — vasodilatory effects studied.
results.translation: Four Substance Decoction [四物湯, Japanese: Shimotsu-to; Chinese: Si Wu Tang] — One of the foundational formulas for tonifying the blood [補血] in the Kampo [Japanese traditional medicine] tradition. Formula components: Toki [当帰, Dang Gui; Angelica sinensis] 12g + Shakuyaku [芍薬, Shao Yao; Paeonia lactiflora] 12g + Senkyu [川芎, Chuan Xiong; Ligusticum chuanxiong] 8g + Jio [地黄, Di Huang; Rehmannia glutinosa] 16g. Commentary of Yoshimasu Todo on Shimotsu-to: This formula is indicated for patterns of blood deficiency [血虚], presenting clinically as pallor of the complexion, dizziness, palpitations, menstrual irregularity, and dryness of the skin. In Japan, the formula was refined with proportions differing from the original Chinese prescription, on the basis of direct clinical observation. The emphasis of the Koho [古方, 'Ancient Formula'] school upon immediate clinical observation and the modification of classical formulas is documented in Yoshimasu Todo's works Yakucho [薬徴] and Ruijuho [類聚方]. Modern pharmacological research on the individual components: Angelica sinensis (active constituent: ferulic acid) — anti-platelet aggregation activity, blood-moving properties; Rehmannia glutinosa — studied for blood sugar regulation; Ligusticum chuanxiong — vasodilatory effects under investigation.

entities_mentioned.name: 四物湯 / Shimotsu-to / Si Wu Tang (Four Substance Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound herbal formula; no single botanical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical composite blood-tonifying formula originating in Chinese medicine and adapted in Japanese Kampo practice. Indicated for blood deficiency [血虚] patterns: pallor, dizziness, palpitations, menstrual irregularity, dry skin. Proportions vary between Chinese and Japanese traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 当帰 / Toki / Dang Gui
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used as blood tonic and emmenagogue. Active constituent ferulic acid associated with anti-platelet and vasodilatory activity. Dose in this formula: 12g. Preparation: decocted.

entities_mentioned.name: 芍薬 / Shakuyaku / Shao Yao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used to nourish blood and moderate spasm. Dose in this formula: 12g. Preparation: decocted.

entities_mentioned.name: 川芎 / Senkyu / Chuan Xiong
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong
entities_mentioned.notes: Rhizome; used to invigorate blood and promote circulation. Modern research documents vasodilatory effects. Dose in this formula: 8g. Preparation: decocted.

entities_mentioned.name: 地黄 / Jio / Di Huang
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used to nourish yin and blood. Modern research suggests effects on blood sugar regulation. Dose in this formula: 16g (heaviest component). Preparation: decocted; may be used raw or prepared [熟地黄] depending on clinical intent.

entities_mentioned.name: Yoshimasu Todo
entities_mentioned.notes: Prominent physician of the Japanese Koho (Ancient Formula) school [古方派]; author of Yakucho [薬徴] and Ruijuho [類聚方], foundational texts documenting clinical observation-based formula use and modification.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a scholarly/editorial composite rather than a single period primary source; it blends classical formula description, historical attribution to Yoshimasu Todo, and modern pharmacological annotations. The Koho school reference and the works Yakucho and Ruijuho are historically documented; these are 18th-century Japanese texts. The modern pharmacological notes (ferulic acid, blood sugar, vasodilation) are 20th–21st century research glosses appended to the classical entry and should not be attributed to the historical primary source. Dose proportions (12:12:8:16) reflect a commonly cited Japanese Kampo variant; Chinese classical proportions differ. The condition/ailment treated is blood deficiency [血虚] with symptoms: pale complexion, dizziness, palpitations, menstrual irregularity, dry skin. Preparation method: aqueous decoction [湯, -to/-tang].

results.title_inferred: Hachimi-jio-gan (Eight-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill): Kampo Kidney-Tonifying Formula
results.original_text: 八味地黄丸 (Hachimi-jio-gan) — Eight-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill. Foundational Kampo kidney-tonifying formula. Components: 地黄 (Rehmannia) 6g + 山薬 (Dioscoria) 3g + 山茱萸 (Cornus fruit) 3g + 茯苓 (Poria) 3g + 沢瀉 (Alisma) 3g + 牡丹皮 (Moutan bark) 3g + 桂枝 (Cinnamon twig) 1g + 附子 (Aconite, processed) 1g. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) as adaptation of Chinese classical formula. Modern Kampo use: frequent urination, lower back weakness, cold extremities, age-related decline in men. Clinical trials for: overactive bladder (positive RCT evidence), age-related cognitive decline, diabetic neuropathy. One of the most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas in Japan's national health insurance system. Annual prescriptions in Japan: millions of defined daily doses.
results.translation: Eight-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill [八味地黄丸, Hachimi-jio-gan] — A foundational Kampo [traditional Japanese medicine] formula for tonifying the kidneys [補腎]. Composition: Rehmannia [地黄, jio] 6g + Chinese Yam [山薬, Dioscorea] 3g + Cornelian Cherry fruit [山茱萸, Cornus officinalis] 3g + Poria fungus [茯苓] 3g + Water Plantain rhizome [沢瀉, Alisma] 3g + Tree Peony root bark [牡丹皮, Moutan] 3g + Cinnamon twig [桂枝] 1g + Processed Aconite root [附子, Aconitum] 1g. This formula is documented in the Ishinhō [医心方] (compiled 984 CE) as a Japanese adaptation of a classical Chinese formula. In contemporary Kampo clinical practice it is prescribed for: frequent urination, lumbar weakness [lower back debility], cold extremities, and age-related physical decline, particularly in men. It has been the subject of clinical trials addressing: overactive bladder (for which positive randomized controlled trial evidence exists), age-related cognitive decline, and diabetic neuropathy. It ranks among the most commonly prescribed Kampo formulas within Japan's national health insurance reimbursement system, with annual prescriptions numbering in the millions of defined daily doses.

entities_mentioned.name: 地黄 (Jio / Rehmannia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC. — root, typically prepared [processed as sheng di huang or shu di huang]
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal ingredient; 6g dose is the largest in the formula, consistent with its role as the chief [君薬] herb. Context does not specify raw vs. prepared form, though prepared Rehmannia is standard in this formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 山薬 (Sanyaku / Dioscorea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea japonica Thunb. or Dioscorea opposita Thunb. — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Source text spells 'Dioscoria'; corrected silently to 'Dioscorea'. Chinese yam, used as a spleen and kidney tonic.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Sanshuyu / Cornus fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc. — dried fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Cornelian cherry; astringent kidney and liver tonic.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bukuryo / Poria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns (syn. Poria cocos F.A.Wolf) — sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: A wood-decay fungus used as a calming and diuretic agent in classical formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 沢瀉 (Takusha / Alisma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma orientale (Sam.) Juz. — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Water plantain; diuretic component of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡丹皮 (Botanpi / Moutan bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia × suffruticosa Andrews — root bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Tree peony root cortex; used for clearing heat and promoting blood circulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 (Keishi / Cinnamon twig)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl — young branch/twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here at low dose (1g) as a yang-warming agent; distinguishes this formula from the six-ingredient Liuwei Dihuang Wan, which lacks cinnamon and aconite.

entities_mentioned.name: 附子 (Bushi / Aconite, processed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux — lateral root, processed [炮附子]
entities_mentioned.notes: Highly toxic in raw form; processing (炮製, hōsei) is essential to reduce aconitine content to safe levels. The low dose (1g) is standard in classical formulation. Use should be flagged in any modern pharmacovigilance context.

entities_mentioned.name: 八味地黄丸 (Hachimi-jio-gan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound Kampo formula — no single botanical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Derived from the Chinese formula Jinkui Shenqi Wan [金匱腎気丸] of Zhang Zhongjing (Han dynasty). The Japanese adaptation documented in the Ishinhō (984 CE) by Tanba no Yasuyori is one of the earliest textual records of this formula in Japan.
