The source text is already largely in English with embedded classical Chinese/Japanese characters; translation has expanded and clarified all technical terms, corrected 'Dioscoria' to 'Dioscorea', and added bracketed original-language terms throughout. 2. The Ishinhō [医心方] (984 CE) is the oldest extant Japanese medical encyclopedia and its citation lends strong historical authority to this formula's provenance. 3. The processed Aconite (附子) ingredient carries significant toxicological relevance; any reproduction of this entry in a materia medica context should include a caution regarding preparation requirements and regulatory status, which varies by jurisdiction. 4. The formula's inclusion in Japan's national health insurance system (保険適用) since 1976 reflects high-level institutional validation and distinguishes it from many traditional preparations lacking regulatory standing.

results.title_inferred: Toki (Dang Gui / Japanese Angelica): Uses, Properties, and Botanical Identity
results.original_text: 当帰 (Toki / Dang Gui) — Angelica acutiloba (Japanese species distinct from Chinese A. sinensis). Honzo Wamyo: 当帰、和名 也末止己呂。甘辛、温。主咳逆上気、温瘧寒熱洒洒在皮膚中、婦人漏下絶子、諸悪瘡瘍、金創。
results.translation: Toki [当帰, also known by the Chinese name Dang Gui]. Japanese name: Yamatorokoro [也末止己呂]. Flavor: sweet-pungent; thermal quality: warm. Principal indications: cough with rebellious qi [counterflow ascent of breath]; warm malaria [温瘧] with alternating cold and heat pervading the skin; women's uterine spotting [漏下, lit. 'dripping downward'] accompanied by infertility; all malign sores and ulcers [諸悪瘡瘍]; and wounds from metal implements [金創, i.e., cuts or punctures from bladed or metal weapons/tools]. Note: Japanese Kampo [traditional Japanese medicine] employs Angelica acutiloba rather than the Chinese Angelica sinensis — a deliberate botanical distinction developed during Japan's Edo period of relative isolation — reflecting Japanese clinical observation that the local species possessed distinct properties. Active compounds include ferulic acid, Z-ligustilide, and phthalides. The herb remains a major gynecological remedy in contemporary Kampo practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 当帰 / Toki / Dang Gui
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica acutiloba (Japanese Kampo usage); cf. Angelica sinensis (Chinese usage)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo records the Japanese vernacular name as Yamatorokoro [也末止己呂]. The deliberate substitution of A. acutiloba for A. sinensis is a documented feature of Edo-period Japanese botanical scholarship. Active compounds include ferulic acid, Z-ligustilide, and phthalides.

entities_mentioned.name: 温瘧 (warm malaria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely corresponds to a febrile, intermittent illness with chills and heat effusion; possibly malaria (Plasmodium spp.) or other periodic febrile syndrome
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese medicine category; 'alternating cold and heat in the skin' suggests an intermittent or remittent fever pattern.

entities_mentioned.name: 婦人漏下絶子 (women's uterine spotting with infertility)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uterine bleeding disorders (e.g., metrorrhagia, abnormal uterine bleeding) associated with infertility
entities_mentioned.notes: 漏下 [lou xia] refers to chronic, dripping uterine bleeding below the normal menstrual cycle; 絶子 indicates failure to conceive or bear children.

entities_mentioned.name: 金創 (metal wounds)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lacerations, cuts, or puncture wounds caused by metal implements (blades, weapons, tools)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard category in classical East Asian materia medica for traumatic wounds; Toki's use here likely relates to its blood-moving and wound-healing properties.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a composite entry combining: (1) a transcription of the classical Honzo Wamyo [本草和名] passage in Literary Chinese/kanbun, (2) an inline English paraphrase, and (3) modern pharmacological and historical commentary. The classical Chinese portion has been translated directly; the appended English commentary has been incorporated into the translation. The Japanese phonetic gloss 也末止己呂 (Yamatorokoro) is rendered in man'yōgana [phonetic kanji] and has been transcribed accordingly. It should be noted that Yamatorokoro is the Honzo Wamyo's vernacular gloss and may differ from later Edo-period or modern Japanese names for this plant. The source's claim that A. acutiloba was a 'deliberate' Edo-period substitution is plausible but should be verified against specialist Kampo historiography.

results.title_inferred: Boi-ogi-to (Stephania and Astragalus Decoction): Formula Composition, Indications, and Clinical Evidence
results.original_text: 防已黄耆湯 (Boi-ogi-to) — Stephania and Astragalus Decoction. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) and standard Kampo formulary. Components: 防已 (Boii/Stephania tetrandra) 5g + 黄耆 (Ougi/Astragalus) 5g + 白术 (Byakujutsu/Atractylodes) 3g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Glycyrrhiza) 1.5g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Zingiber) 1g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 3g. Traditional indications: sweating, heaviness, joint swelling particularly in the knees, knee osteoarthritis. Modern clinical evidence: multiple Japanese RCTs for knee osteoarthritis showing significant symptom reduction. One of the best-studied Kampo formulas for musculoskeletal conditions. Mechanism: stephania tetrandrine (anti-inflammatory alkaloid) + astragalus immune modulation + atractylodes diuretic effect for joint fluid accumulation.
results.translation: Boi-ogi-to [防已黄耆湯, 'Stephania and Astragalus Decoction']: A compound decoction [tō] recorded in the Ishinhō [医心方, compiled 984 CE] and included in the standard Kampo [Japanese classical herbal medicine] formulary. Composition: Bōi [防已, Stephania tetrandra root] 5 g; Ōgi [黄耆, Astragalus membranaceus root] 5 g; Byakujutsu [白术, Atractylodes macrocephala or A. japonica rhizome] 3 g; Kanzō [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis root, liquorice] 1.5 g; Shōkyō [生姜, Zingiber officinale, fresh ginger] 1 g; Taisō [大棗, Ziziphus jujuba fruit, jujube] 3 g. Traditional indications: spontaneous sweating [jishukan], sensation of bodily heaviness, articular swelling with particular involvement of the knees, and knee osteoarthritis. Contemporary clinical evidence: several Japanese randomised controlled trials for knee osteoarthritis have demonstrated significant reduction of symptoms. This formula is among the most thoroughly investigated Kampo prescriptions for musculoskeletal disorders. Proposed mechanism of action: tetrandrine from Stephania (an anti-inflammatory alkaloid); immunomodulatory action of Astragalus; diuretic action of Atractylodes reducing intra-articular fluid accumulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 防已 (Bōi / Boii)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stephania tetrandra S. Moore (family Menispermaceae); root
entities_mentioned.notes: Active constituent tetrandrine is a bisbenzylisoquinoline alkaloid with documented anti-inflammatory properties. Note: 防已 may occasionally refer to Sinomenium acutum in some regional traditions; context here specifies S. tetrandra.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄耆 (Ōgi / Ougi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bunge or A. membranaceus var. mongholicus (family Fabaceae); root
entities_mentioned.notes: Classic tonic herb attributed with immunomodulatory and adaptogenic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 白术 (Byakujutsu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. or Atractylodes japonica Koidz. ex Kitam. (family Asteraceae); rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for its diuretic and stomachic properties; helps reduce excess fluid [dampness] in Kampo theory.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Kanzō / Kanzo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or G. glabra L. (family Fabaceae); root and stolon [liquorice]
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonising herb in Kampo formulae; anti-inflammatory and spasmolytic properties also documented.

entities_mentioned.name: 生姜 (Shōkyō / Shokyo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae); fresh rhizome [ginger]
entities_mentioned.notes: Adjuvant ingredient; aids absorption, reduces gastrointestinal adverse effects of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 (Taiso)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (family Rhamnaceae); mature fruit [jujube / Chinese date]
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard harmonising and tonifying adjuvant in classical Chinese and Kampo formulae.

entities_mentioned.name: 防已黄耆湯 (Boi-ogi-to)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound Kampo decoction; no single-plant identification applicable
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula name romanised variously as Boiogito, Bōi-ōgi-tō, or Fang Ji Huang Qi Tang (Chinese). Documented clinical use for knee osteoarthritis with RCT support in Japan.

results.translator_notes: The source text is already predominantly in English with embedded Japanese/Chinese terms; translation has formalised the phrasing, expanded abbreviations, and added botanical family designations for scholarly precision. The romanisation 'Boi-ogi-to' reflects a simplified Hepburn transcription; standard modern Hepburn would render this Bōi-ōgi-tō. 'RCT' (randomised controlled trial) is retained as standard medical terminology. The claim regarding multiple Japanese RCTs is a factual assertion in the source and has not been independently verified here; downstream editors should confirm citations. Atractylodes species identification (macrocephala vs. japonica) varies by Japanese pharmacopoeial edition; both are acceptable referents for Byakujutsu depending on source period.

results.title_inferred: Ginseng (Ninjin / Ren Shen) — Honzo Wamyo Entry with Kampo and Pharmacological Notes
results.original_text: 人参 (Ninjin / Ren Shen) — Panax ginseng. 人参、和名 久留万久佐。甘、微寒。主補五臓、安精神、定魂魄、止驚悸、除邪気、明目開心益智。久服軽身延年。
results.translation: Ginseng [人参, Ninjin / Ren Shen] — Japanese name: Kurumakusa [久留万久佐]. Flavour: sweet; nature: mildly cold. Primary indications: tonifies the five viscera [五臓, i.e., heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney], calms the spirit [精神], stabilises the corporeal soul [魄, pò] and the ethereal soul [魂, hún], arrests fright and palpitations [驚悸], eliminates pathogenic [evil] qi [邪気], brightens the eyes, opens the heart, and augments wisdom and intelligence. Prolonged use lightens the body and prolongs life. Kampo [Japanese classical medicine] applications: regarded as the principal herb for supplementing qi; administered at a reduced dose compared to Chinese tradition, and combined with warming herbs in order to temper its cold nature. Active constituents: ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, and Re — possessing adaptogenic, neuroprotective, and anti-fatigue properties. More than 3,000 modern research papers exist on this substance. Clinical evidence supports its use for fatigue, immune modulation, and cognitive function in elderly patients.

entities_mentioned.name: 人参 / Ninjin / Ren Shen (Kurumakusa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Japanese vernacular name Kurumakusa [久留万久佐] is recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, ca. 918 CE), one of Japan's earliest materia medica texts. The Kampo tradition typically uses smaller doses than the Chinese and pairs ginseng with warming agents such as dried ginger or aconite to counterbalance its mildly cold nature. The ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, and Re are the primary pharmacologically active saponins identified in modern research.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a composite entry: the classical Chinese-character passage derives from or is closely modelled on the Honzo Wamyo and ultimately on the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] tradition, while the final two sentences are modern editorial annotations summarising Kampo practice and contemporary pharmacological research. These sections have been translated accordingly but should be understood as distinct strata of the document. The terms 魂 (hún, ethereal soul) and 魄 (pò, corporeal soul) are preserved with brackets as they carry specific cosmological meanings in classical Chinese medicine that have no exact English equivalents. 'Lightens the body' [軽身] is a conventional formula in Chinese materia medica indicating enhanced vitality and agility rather than literal weight loss.

results.title_inferred: Shakuyaku-kanzo-to (Peony and Licorice Decoction): Formula, Historical Basis, and Clinical Evidence
results.original_text: 芍薬甘草湯 (Shakuyaku-kanzo-to) — Peony and Licorice Decoction. One of the most prescribed Kampo formulas in modern Japan. Components: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Paeonia) 20g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Glycyrrhiza) 20g. Documented in Jinkikyo (Jin Gui Yao Lue, classical Chinese), adapted in Japanese Kampo with higher doses than Chinese tradition. Traditional indications: muscle spasms, cramping, abdominal pain, leg cramps. Modern clinical applications with RCT evidence: (1) muscle cramps in hemodialysis patients — multiple Japanese RCTs showing significant reduction in cramp frequency; (2) dysmenorrhea — multiple RCTs showing comparable efficacy to NSAIDs; (3) PCOS-related androgen excess — several trials showing reduction in testosterone levels through paeoniflorin's anti-androgen mechanism; (4) chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy — used in Japanese oncology. One of the most evidence-rich Kampo formulas.
results.translation: Shakuyaku-kanzo-to [芍薬甘草湯], known in English as 'Peony and Licorice Decoction,' is among the most frequently prescribed Kampo [traditional Japanese herbal medicine] formulas in contemporary Japan. It is a two-ingredient preparation composed of Shakuyaku [芍薬; Paeonia lactiflora, white peony root] at 20g and Kanzo [甘草; Glycyrrhiza uralensis or G. glabra, licorice root] at 20g. The formula is documented in the Jinkikyo [仁機経; the Japanese reading of the classical Chinese text Jin Gui Yao Lue, 'Essential Prescriptions of the Golden Cabinet'], and has been adapted within the Japanese Kampo tradition at notably higher dosages than those employed in Chinese herbal practice. Traditional therapeutic indications include muscle spasms, cramping pains, abdominal pain, and leg cramps. In modern clinical usage, the formula has been evaluated in randomized controlled trials (RCTs) for the following applications: (1) muscle cramps in patients undergoing hemodialysis, with multiple Japanese RCTs demonstrating significant reduction in cramp frequency; (2) dysmenorrhea [painful menstruation], with multiple RCTs indicating efficacy comparable to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs); (3) androgen excess associated with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), with several trials demonstrating reduction in serum testosterone levels, attributed to the anti-androgenic mechanism of paeoniflorin, an active constituent of Paeonia; and (4) chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, for which the formula is employed in Japanese oncological practice. Shakuyaku-kanzo-to is regarded as one of the Kampo formulas with the strongest evidentiary base in modern clinical medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (white peony root; Paeoniae Radix Alba)
entities_mentioned.notes: Active constituent paeoniflorin is highlighted for anti-androgenic properties. Standard component of Kampo and TCM formularies.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Kanzo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. or Glycyrrhiza glabra L. (licorice root; Glycyrrhizae Radix)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a harmonizing agent in many Kampo and TCM formulas; contains glycyrrhizin and glycyrrhetinic acid. Higher doses as used in Kampo carry a risk of pseudohyperaldosteronism with prolonged use.

entities_mentioned.name: Jinkikyo (Jin Gui Yao Lue)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jin Gui Yao Lue [金匱要略], classical Chinese medical text attributed to Zhang Zhongjing (c. 3rd century CE)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Jinkikyo' is the Japanese on'yomi reading of 金匱要略. The formula Shakuyaku-kanzo-to derives directly from this foundational classical source.

entities_mentioned.name: Paeoniflorin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeoniflorin (C₂₃H₂₈O₁₁); a monoterpene glycoside and principal bioactive constituent of Paeonia lactiflora
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as the mechanism underlying the anti-androgenic effects observed in PCOS trials. Well-characterized pharmacologically.

results.translator_notes: The source text is already substantially in English with Japanese terms integrated; translation has formalized and expanded the clinical and historical context. The romanization 'Jinkikyo' for 金匱要略 is an unusual Japanese reading; the standard Japanese title is Kinki Yōryaku (金匱要略), though 'Jinkikyo' may reflect an older or variant reading. This should be flagged for editorial review. Dosage discrepancy between Japanese Kampo and Chinese TCM practice (higher doses in Kampo) is noted and preserved as clinically significant. Risk of adverse effects from high-dose Kanzo (pseudohyperaldosteronism) is not mentioned in the source but may be worth noting in a pharmacological annotation.

results.title_inferred: Sho-saiko-to (Minor Bupleurum Decoction): Classical Kampo Formula — Composition, Indications, and Pharmacovigilance
results.original_text: 小柴胡湯 (Sho-saiko-to) — Minor Bupleurum Decoction. Classical Kampo formula documented from earliest Japanese adaptation of Chinese medicine. Components: 柴胡 (Saikou/Bupleurum) 7g + 半夏 (Hange/Pinellia) 5g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Ginger) 4g + 黄芩 (Ogon/Scutellaria) 3g + 人参 (Ninjin/Ginseng) 3g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Licorice) 2g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 3g. Traditional indications: alternating fever and chills, bitter taste in mouth, dry throat, nausea, chest and hypochondriac fullness. Modern applications: extensively studied in Japan for chronic hepatitis B and C — multiple large RCTs showing hepatoprotective effects, reduction of hepatitis B surface antigen levels, and reduction in liver cancer risk in cirrhotic patients. Mechanism: saikosaponins modulate NF-kB inflammatory pathway, Scutellaria baicalensis (baicalin) — antiviral and anti-inflammatory. Contraindication discovered 1994: should not be combined with interferon — severe interstitial pneumonia risk. This contraindication discovery from post-marketing surveillance in Japan is a model for traditional medicine pharmacovigilance.
results.translation: Sho-saiko-to [Small Bupleurum Decoction; Chinese: Xiao Chai Hu Tang] — Minor Bupleurum Decoction. This is a classical Kampo [Japanese traditional medicine] compound formula, documented from the earliest period of Japanese adaptation of Chinese medical tradition. Constituent ingredients and standard dosages: Saikou [柴胡, Bupleurum root] 7 g; Hange [半夏, Pinellia rhizome] 5 g; Shokyo [生姜, fresh Ginger rhizome] 4 g; Ogon [黄芩, Scutellaria root] 3 g; Ninjin [人参, Ginseng root] 3 g; Kanzo [甘草, Licorice root] 2 g; Taiso [大棗, Jujube fruit] 3 g. Traditional indications [as recognised in classical Kampo nosology]: alternating fever and chills [a 'Shao-yang stage' [少陽病] presentation], bitter taste in the mouth, dry throat, nausea, and a sensation of fullness in the chest and hypochondriac [subcostal] regions. Modern clinical applications: the formula has been extensively investigated in Japan for the treatment of chronic hepatitis B and C. Multiple large randomised controlled trials have demonstrated hepatoprotective effects, reduction in hepatitis B surface antigen [HBsAg] levels, and a measurable reduction in the risk of hepatocellular carcinoma [liver cancer] in cirrhotic patients. Proposed pharmacological mechanisms: saikosaponins [the principal active constituents of Bupleurum] modulate the NF-κB inflammatory signalling pathway; baicalin, derived from Scutellaria baicalensis [Chinese skullcap], exerts antiviral and anti-inflammatory actions. Important contraindication identified in 1994: co-administration with interferon [α-interferon, used in antiviral therapy] is contraindicated owing to the risk of severe interstitial pneumonia. The identification of this contraindication through post-marketing surveillance [pharmacovigilance] in Japan has been cited as a paradigmatic example of safety monitoring for traditional medicine products.

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 / Saikou / Bupleurum (Sho-saiko-to formula as a whole)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sho-saiko-to = Xiao Chai Hu Tang; multi-herb Kampo/TCM decoction formula
entities_mentioned.notes: The formula originates in the Shang Han Lun (c. 200 CE) and is among the most extensively studied Kampo formulas in modern Japanese clinical research.

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 / Saikou / Bupleurum root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (Apiaceae); also B. chinense DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal herb of the formula; saikosaponins are its major bioactive constituents and are held responsible for immunomodulatory and hepatoprotective effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 半夏 / Hange / Pinellia rhizome
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino (Araceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in processed (processed with ginger or alum) form in Kampo to reduce toxicity; anti-emetic and expectorant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 生姜 / Shokyo / Fresh Ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used fresh (生姜) in this formula; serves as an adjuvant to moderate Pinellia's toxicity and to harmonise the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄芩 / Ogon / Scutellaria root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Source of baicalin and baicalein; documented antiviral (including anti-hepatitis B), anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 人参 / Ninjin / Ginseng root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonic and adaptogenic herb; in this formula functions to support 'Zheng Qi' [vital energy/immune defence] and tonify the spleen and stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 / Kanzo / Licorice root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC. or G. glabra L. (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonising herb in the formula; glycyrrhizin is independently recognised for hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 / Taiso / Jujube fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (Rhamnaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nutritive tonic; used to moderate the formula's properties and protect the gastrointestinal tract.

results.translator_notes: The source text is already substantially in English with embedded Japanese/Chinese terminology; translation effort here consists principally of expanding, clarifying, and contextualising the technical and historical content for a scholarly Materia Medica audience. The 1994 interferon–Sho-saiko-to interaction is a well-documented pharmacovigilance event in Japan; the resulting regulatory action (contraindication labelling by the Japanese Ministry of Health and Welfare) is historically significant for evidence-based integration of traditional medicines. The condition description 'alternating fever and chills, bitter taste in mouth, dry throat, nausea, chest and hypochondriac fullness' corresponds precisely to the classical Shang Han Lun description of the Shao-yang [少陽] disease stage, which is the primary indication for this formula in classical sources. 'Hypochondriac fullness' here refers to subcostal / lateral costal fullness [胸脇苦満, kyokyo-kuman in Kampo], a key diagnostic sign. NF-κB has been rendered NF-κB (retaining the standard biochemical symbol) rather than 'NF-kB' as in the source.

results.title_inferred: Kakkonto (Kudzu Combination Decoction): Formula Composition, Traditional Indications, and Modern Applications
results.original_text: 葛根湯 (Kakkon-to) — Kudzu Combination Decoction. Most widely recognised Kampo formula in Japan. Components: 葛根 (Kakkon/Pueraria lobata root) 8g + 麻黄 (Mao/Ephedra) 4g + 桂枝 (Keishi/Cinnamon) 3g + 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Peony) 3g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 4g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Ginger) 4g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Licorice) 2g. Traditional indications: early stage cold or flu with stiffness of the back of the neck and upper back, fever without sweating, aversion to cold. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) and standard Kampo practice throughout Japanese history. Modern applications: most frequently prescribed Kampo formula for common cold. Over-the-counter availability in Japan. Active in early viral infection stage. Puerarin (from kudzu root): vasodilatory, anti-platelet effects studied for cardiovascular applications. Used in Japanese emergency medicine for early-stage fever. Multiple observational studies showing shorter duration of common cold symptoms.
results.translation: Kakkonto [葛根湯, 'Kudzu Combination Decoction']: The most widely recognised formula [処方, shohō] in the Japanese Kampo [漢方] tradition. Composition: Kakkon [葛根, Pueraria lobata root] 8g; Mao [麻黄, Ephedra herb] 4g; Keishi [桂枝, Cinnamomum cassia twig] 3g; Shakuyaku [芍薬, Paeonia lactiflora root] 3g; Taiso [大棗, Ziziphus jujuba fruit] 4g; Shokyo [生姜, fresh Zingiber officinale rhizome] 4g; Kanzo [甘草, Glycyrrhiza uralensis root] 2g. Traditional indications [適応症]: early-stage cold or influenza presenting with rigidity of the nape of the neck and upper back, fever without perspiration, and aversion to cold [悪寒, okan]. First documented in the Ishinhō [医心方] (compiled 984 CE) and employed continuously in standard Kampo clinical practice throughout Japanese medical history. Contemporary applications: the most frequently prescribed Kampo formula for the common cold; available without prescription [over-the-counter] in Japan. Indicated during the early stage of viral infection. The constituent compound puerarin [プエラリン], derived from kudzu root [葛根], has been investigated for vasodilatory and anti-platelet properties with potential cardiovascular applications. The formula is also employed in Japanese emergency medicine settings for early-stage febrile illness. Multiple observational studies report a reduction in the duration of common cold symptoms following its administration.

entities_mentioned.name: 葛根 (Kakkon) / Pueraria lobata root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata (Willd.) Sanjappa & Pradeep — kudzu root
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal herb of the formula; contains the isoflavone puerarin, studied for vasodilatory and anti-platelet cardiovascular effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻黄 (Mao) / Ephedra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (or related Ephedra spp.) — ma-huang herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Source of ephedrine alkaloids; diaphoretic and bronchodilatory properties relevant to the anti-febrile action of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 (Keishi) / Cinnamon twig
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl — cassia cinnamon twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Warming, circulatory-stimulating herb; used in Kampo as twig [枝, shi] rather than bark to differentiate from the stronger Keihi [桂皮].

entities_mentioned.name: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku) / Peony root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. — white peony root
entities_mentioned.notes: Antispasmodic; used here to relieve muscular tension and neck stiffness.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 (Taiso) / Jujube fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. — common jujube
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonising herb in traditional Kampo formulae; also provides mild tonic and nutritive support.

entities_mentioned.name: 生姜 (Shokyo) / Fresh ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe — ginger rhizome (fresh form)
entities_mentioned.notes: Diaphoretic and anti-nausea herb; the fresh form [生姜, shokyo] is distinguished from dried ginger [乾姜, kankyo] in Kampo dosing.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Kanzo) / Licorice root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC. — Chinese licorice root
entities_mentioned.notes: Universal harmonising herb in Kampo; anti-inflammatory and demulcent; moderates the harshness of other formula components.

entities_mentioned.name: Kakkonto [葛根湯] — compound formula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Standardised Kampo polypharmaceutical preparation (TJ-1 in Tsumura numbering)
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula-level entry. First recorded in the Ishinhō (984 CE); ultimately traceable to the Chinese Shanghanlun [傷寒論] classical tradition. Available OTC in Japan; subject to multiple modern clinical observational studies for common cold.

results.translator_notes: The source text is already substantially in English with Japanese terms and romanisations embedded; translation work here primarily involves rendering the Japanese-language terms into properly contextualised scholarly English, clarifying Kampo-specific technical vocabulary, and organising the botanical identifications. 'Ishinhō' [医心方], compiled by Tamba no Yasuyori in 984 CE, is Japan's oldest extant medical encyclopaedia and the earliest Japanese documentary source cited. The distinction between Keishi [桂枝, cinnamon twig] and Keihi [桂皮, cinnamon bark] is clinically significant in Kampo and has been preserved in the translation notes. Puerarin's cardiovascular applications are noted as 'studied' — the translation preserves this appropriately cautious framing from the source.

results.title_inferred: Gardenia Fruit (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi) in Honzo Wamyo: Properties, Indications, and Active Compounds
results.original_text: 山梔子 (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi) — Gardenia jasminoides fruit. Honzo Wamyo: 山梔子、和名 久知奈之。苦、寒。主五内邪気、胃中熱気、面赤酒皰皶鼻、白癩赤癩瘡瘍。
results.translation: Sanshishi [Mountain Gardenia Fruit; Chin. Zhi Zi], Japanese vernacular name: Kuchinashi [久知奈之]. Flavor: bitter. Thermal nature: cold. Indicated for: pathogenic qi [邪気, evil qi] in the five viscera [五内, i.e., the five organ systems]; heat qi [熱気] in the stomach; redness of the face and rosacea [酒皰皶鼻, lit. 'wine-blotch red nose,' i.e., acne rosacea]; white leprosy and red leprosy [白癩・赤癩, likely referring to distinct forms of leprous or depigmenting skin disease]; sores and ulcers [瘡瘍]. Principal use in Kampo medicine: a heat-clearing [清熱] herb for inflammatory conditions. Active constituent of pharmacological note: genipin, studied for neuroprotective effects (anti-inflammatory activity in the central nervous system), hepatoprotection, antidepressant activity (in animal models), and antidiabetic activity. Cross-traditional note: gardenia fruit is employed with essentially identical indications in both Chinese medicine and the Japanese Kampo tradition, constituting a within-East-Asian convergence signal for this drug's therapeutic profile.

entities_mentioned.name: 山梔子 (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi; Japanese vernacular: Kuchinashi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides Ellis, fruit (family Rubiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Explicitly identified in source as Gardenia jasminoides fruit. The vernacular name Kuchinashi (久知奈之) is the standard Japanese name for this plant, consistent with Honzo Wamyo orthography.

entities_mentioned.name: Genipin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipin (CAS 6902-77-8), an iridoid aglycone derived from geniposide found in Gardenia jasminoides fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Active compound cited in the source annotation. Well-documented in modern pharmacological literature for hepatoprotective, neuroprotective, antidepressant (animal models), and antidiabetic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 五内邪気 (pathogenic qi in the five viscera)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese medical concept; no direct modern biomedical equivalent. Refers to pathological states affecting the five organ systems (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney).

entities_mentioned.name: 酒皰皶鼻 (wine-blotch red nose / rosacea)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acne rosacea or rhinophyma
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term for facial redness and nasal pustular eruption associated with alcohol; modern correlate is most likely rosacea.

entities_mentioned.name: 白癩・赤癩 (white leprosy / red leprosy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly leprosy (Mycobacterium leprae) or other chronic depigmenting/erythematous skin diseases
entities_mentioned.notes: These classical categories do not map cleanly onto modern dermatological nosology. 白癩 may correspond to depigmenting conditions (possibly vitiligo or tuberculoid leprosy); 赤癩 to erythematous forms. Identification remains uncertain.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a hybrid scholarly annotation combining: (1) a classical Chinese-language materia medica entry from the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, c. 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito], (2) modern pharmacological commentary, and (3) a methodological 'convergence signal' note. The translation separates these layers for clarity. The Japanese vernacular transcription 久知奈之 uses Man'yogana phonetic rendering of 'Kuchinashi,' the standard Japanese name still in use today. The thermal/flavor classification (苦・寒, bitter-cold) is a standard Chinese medicine drug property designation and has been preserved with bracketed explanation.

results.title_inferred: Byakujutsu (White Atractylodes): Honzo Wamyo Entry and Kampo Clinical Notes
results.original_text: 白术 (Byakujutsu / Bai Zhu) — Atractylodes japonica (Japanese species) / A. macrocephala (Chinese species). Kampo uses Japanese species — distinct from Chinese use, representing independent clinical development. Honzo Wamyo: 白术、和名 於介良。苦、温。主風寒湿痺死肌、痙疸、止汗除熱、消食。Byakujutsu, Japanese name Okera. Bitter, warm. Main: wind-cold-damp obstruction with insensate flesh, spasms and jaundice, stops sweating, eliminates heat, promotes food digestion. The Japanese Kampo preference for A. japonica over A. macrocephala developed during the Edo period of isolation and is documented by Koho school physicians as reflecting genuinely different clinical properties. Active: atractylodin, beta-eudesmol — anti-inflammatory, gastroprotective, immune-modulating. Major digestive herb in Kampo formulas.
results.translation:
Byakujutsu [白术; Japanese: Byakujutsu; Chinese: Bai Zhu] — species: Atractylodes japonica (Japanese) or A. macrocephala (Chinese). Kampo medicine employs the Japanese species, representing an independent clinical tradition distinct from Chinese practice.

From the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 'Japanese Names for Materia Medica']: Byakujutsu; Japanese vernacular name: Okera [於介良]. Flavor: bitter. Thermal nature: warm. Primary indications: obstruction [bi 痺] due to wind, cold, and dampness with insensate or necrotic flesh [死肌]; convulsions/spasms [痙]; jaundice [疸]; arrests spontaneous sweating; eliminates [pathogenic] heat; promotes digestion and dispels food stagnation.

The Japanese Kampo preference for A. japonica over A. macrocephala emerged during the Edo period of national isolation and is attested by physicians of the Koho [古方, 'Ancient Formula'] school, who regarded the two species as possessing genuinely distinct clinical properties. Known active constituents include atractylodin and beta-eudesmol, with documented anti-inflammatory, gastroprotective, and immune-modulating activity. Byakujutsu functions as a principal digestive herb in numerous Kampo compound formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Byakujutsu / Bai Zhu (白术)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes japonica Koidz. (Japanese Kampo usage) / Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (Chinese usage); family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo gives the Japanese vernacular name as Okera (於介良), which in modern Japanese botanical usage refers specifically to A. japonica. The distinction between the Japanese and Chinese species is clinically significant in Kampo tradition and is explicitly acknowledged in Edo-period Koho school texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Okera (於介良)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes japonica Koidz.
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional Japanese vernacular name recorded in the Honzo Wamyo; still in use in modern Japanese botanical nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: Atractylodin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyacetylene compound isolated from Atractylodes spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: A principal bioactive constituent; documented anti-inflammatory and gastroprotective properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-eudesmol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene alcohol found in Atractylodes spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Immune-modulating and neurological activity documented in modern pharmacological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Koho school (古方派)
entities_mentioned.notes: Influential Edo-period Japanese medical school advocating return to classical Chinese formula texts (Zhang Zhongjing tradition); relevant here as the tradition that documented the clinical differentiation of A. japonica from A. macrocephala.

results.translator_notes: The source text is a composite editorial entry blending (1) the classical Chinese/Japanese Honzo Wamyo citation in Literary Chinese, (2) a romanized Japanese reading, and (3) modern pharmacological and historiographical commentary. These layers have been preserved and distinguished in the translation. The term 死肌 (shi ji), rendered here as 'insensate or necrotic flesh,' literally means 'dead muscle/flesh' and refers in classical Chinese medicine to areas of numbness or sensory loss associated with bi [obstruction] syndrome; this rendering follows standard Kampo and TCM translation conventions. The Honzo Wamyo (918 CE, compiled by Minamoto no Shitagō) is the earliest extant Japanese materia medica glossary and is a primary source of significant scholarly value.
